# RevenProx — full text

> RevenProx is an open-source, high-performance Server-Sent Events (SSE) proxy built in Zig by dotcommoners. It delivers real-time SSE streams to 10M+ concurrent clients across 100+ instances using a brokerless NNG mesh (bloom filters, merkle trees, vector clocks for state sync), with webhook-based JWT authentication (caching + circuit breaker), UUID topic-based routing, and a minimal per-connection memory footprint. License: MIT. Self-hostable.

This is the expanded, single-document version of the RevenProx site content, intended for language models and answer engines. Everything below is accurate to the project; no benchmarks or features beyond what the project documents are claimed.

---

## Overview

RevenProx is an open-source Zig SSE proxy for streaming real-time Server-Sent Events to 10M+ concurrent clients across 100+ instances over a brokerless NNG mesh — with JWT authentication, UUID topic-based routing, and a minimal per-connection memory footprint. It is MIT-licensed and self-hostable.

### The problem: SSE at the scale of the whole internet

Server-Sent Events (SSE) is the simplest way to push a one-way stream of updates from a server to a browser: a long-lived HTTP response that stays open and emits events over time, with automatic client reconnection built in. It is ideal for LLM token streams, live dashboards, notifications, and activity feeds.

But the model has a hard edge: each connection stays open for the client's entire session. A popular application therefore does not have "requests per second," it has millions of connections held open at once. At that scale the cost is not CPU; it is the memory and coordination overhead of every idle-but-open socket, plus the fan-out problem of routing the right event to the right subset of those millions of clients. General-purpose reverse proxies and managed pub/sub services can be pressed into the role, but they either bottleneck on a central broker or bill per message for infrastructure you could own.

### The approach: brokerless mesh, minimal memory

RevenProx sits at the streaming edge and holds the long-lived SSE connections. Instead of routing all events through a central broker, the proxy instances form a fully distributed, brokerless NNG mesh. State — which topics exist and which instances hold subscribers for them — is synchronized peer-to-peer using bloom filters, merkle trees, and vector clocks, so any instance can accept a publish and get it to every subscriber, wherever they are connected, with no single coordinator to overload.

Because it is written in Zig — no garbage collector, no heavyweight runtime — per-connection memory is small and predictable, which is what actually determines how many idle streams a node can hold. That combination is what lets a fleet reach the 10M+ concurrent target.

### Key capabilities

- Massive scale: designed for 10M+ concurrent SSE connections across 100+ proxy instances; capacity grows by adding instances to the mesh.
- Brokerless mesh: no central message broker; state and events flow peer-to-peer across a distributed NNG mesh.
- UUID topic routing: events are routed to subscribers by UUID topic — targeted fan-out, not broadcast.
- JWT authentication: webhook-based token verification with result caching and a circuit breaker to protect the auth upstream.
- Built in Zig: no GC, minimal runtime, low and predictable per-connection memory.
- Self-hostable, MIT-licensed: builds to a single static binary; Docker and Kubernetes deployment guides provided.

### Who it's for

RevenProx is self-hosted infrastructure, not a SaaS. It is for platform, infrastructure, and real-time engineers who run their own streaming edge, need SSE fan-out at internet scale, want to own the data path, and would rather add nodes to a mesh than pay per message to a managed broker.

### Quick start

```
# Clone and build a single static binary
git clone https://github.com/dotcommoners/revenprox.git
cd revenprox
zig build -Doptimize=ReleaseSafe

# Run the proxy
./zig-out/bin/sse-proxy --config config/proxy.toml

# Subscribe a client to a topic (JWT-authenticated)
curl -N "http://localhost:8080/events/my-topic" -H "Authorization: Bearer <jwt>"
```

---

## Architecture

Summary: RevenProx holds long-lived SSE connections at the edge and synchronizes state across a fully distributed, brokerless NNG mesh. Instances agree on which topics exist and where subscribers live using bloom filters, merkle trees, and vector clocks; events are routed to subscribers by UUID topic; and clients are authenticated with webhook-based JWT verification that caches results and trips a circuit breaker to protect the auth upstream. Written in Zig, it holds millions of idle-but-open connections at low, predictable per-connection memory.

### The data path, end to end

1. Connect. A client opens a long-lived SSE request (e.g. GET /events/<topic>) against any proxy instance and passes a JWT.
2. Authenticate. The instance verifies the JWT via a webhook to your auth service, caching the result so repeat connects do not re-hit the upstream.
3. Subscribe. The connection is registered against a UUID topic. Membership for that topic propagates across the mesh so every instance knows a subscriber exists.
4. Publish. An event published to a topic — on any instance — is relayed across the NNG mesh to exactly the instances holding subscribers for that topic.
5. Deliver. Each instance writes the event down its local matching SSE connections. No event travels to an instance that has no subscribers for the topic.

### The brokerless NNG mesh

The defining decision in RevenProx is that there is no central message broker. A broker is the classic scaling ceiling for pub/sub: every message and every subscription passes through it, so it becomes the thing you must over-provision, license, and page someone about. RevenProx removes it entirely. The proxy instances connect to one another directly over NNG (nanomsg-next-gen) to form a peer-to-peer mesh. Any instance can accept a publish and get it to every subscriber across the fleet, so there is no single coordinator to bottleneck — you add throughput by adding instances.

### State synchronization: bloom filters + merkle trees + vector clocks

A brokerless mesh needs every instance to agree on distributed state — which topics exist and which instances currently hold subscribers for them — without a central registry. RevenProx does this with three complementary primitives:

- Bloom filters give each instance a compact, probabilistic summary of the topics it holds subscribers for. Peers exchange these to cheaply answer "could you have subscribers for this topic?" and skip forwarding events to instances that definitely do not — keeping fan-out targeted instead of flooding the mesh.
- Merkle trees let two instances compare their view of shared state by exchanging hashes rather than the full state. Matching root hashes mean "we already agree"; a mismatch lets them walk the tree to find exactly which branches diverged, so reconciliation transfers only the diff.
- Vector clocks order updates across instances without a global clock. When the same topic's membership changes on different nodes, vector clocks establish causal ordering and detect concurrent updates, so the mesh converges on a consistent view instead of races producing contradictory state.

Together these make state sync gossip-friendly and bandwidth-cheap: instances mostly confirm they already agree, and only exchange real data when something has actually changed.

### UUID topic-based routing

Every stream is a UUID topic. Clients subscribe to a topic; publishers publish to a topic; the mesh routes by topic. Using UUIDs gives an effectively unlimited, collision-free topic space — you can mint a topic per user, per session, per document, or per conversation without coordination. Because routing is per-topic, an event only reaches the clients that subscribed to it: targeted fan-out, not broadcast-to-everyone.

### Authentication: webhook JWT + cache + circuit breaker

RevenProx authenticates connections with JWTs, but verification is webhook-based — the proxy calls out to your auth service to validate a token rather than embedding your auth logic. Two mechanisms keep that from becoming a liability at scale: caching (verification results are cached, so a burst of reconnects for an already-seen token does not translate into a burst of calls to your auth service) and a circuit breaker (if the auth upstream starts failing or slowing down, the breaker trips and stops hammering it, protecting a degraded dependency from a thundering herd instead of amplifying the outage).

### Memory model: why Zig

When you hold millions of connections that are mostly idle, cost is governed by per-connection memory, not CPU. RevenProx is written in Zig: no garbage collector, no heavyweight runtime, and manual, predictable memory layout. That keeps per-connection overhead small and predictable, so a node's capacity is a number you can plan a fleet around rather than a GC-pause lottery. This is the property that makes the 10M+ concurrent, 100+ instance target reachable.

### Deployment shape

RevenProx builds to a single static binary with Zig and is self-hosted on your own infrastructure — no managed control plane. Instances discover one another and form the NNG mesh; you scale by running more of them. Docker and Kubernetes deployment guides are provided in the documentation.

---

## Use cases

RevenProx fits any workload that pushes a one-way, real-time event stream to a very large audience over Server-Sent Events. The common thread: many long-lived connections, targeted per-topic fan-out, and no central broker to bottleneck.

- LLM token and agent event streaming: generative apps stream tokens as they are produced — a naturally one-way, long-lived stream per request. With a large audience you have millions of open streams at once. Use a UUID topic per conversation or request, fan model output to the right client with no broker in the path, and hold idle-between-tokens connections cheaply thanks to Zig's low per-connection memory.
- Live dashboards and metrics: publish metric updates to a topic and every open dashboard subscribed to it updates in real time, with targeted routing so a dashboard only receives the streams it subscribed to.
- Real-time notifications and activity feeds: mint a topic per user or channel and deliver events the moment they happen; webhook JWT auth ties each subscription to your identity system so users only receive their own streams.
- Multiplayer and collaboration: a topic per room or document routes updates to exactly its participants; SSE covers the server-to-client broadcast half, with clients POSTing their own changes back over ordinary requests.
- Market data and price feeds: per-symbol or per-market UUID topics route high-frequency feeds to just their subscribers; adding watchers means adding instances rather than scaling a central bus.
- CI/CD logs and build output: relay build output over a topic so watchers see lines as they are emitted, without polling an API or holding connections open on application servers.

When RevenProx is the right tool: the traffic is one-way (server to client); connections are long-lived and numerous; you want to own the data path rather than pay per message; and delivery must be targeted per topic. If you need full-duplex, low-latency bidirectional messaging, WebSockets are the better primitive.

---

## Comparison: RevenProx vs. the alternatives

Choose RevenProx when you want self-hosted, brokerless SSE fan-out at internet scale without per-message fees. Rolling your own gives maximum control but makes you build and operate connection handling, fan-out, and cross-node state. Managed pub/sub (Pusher, Ably, Redis pub/sub, and similar) removes operational burden but bills per message/connection and moves traffic through a third party.

### vs. rolling your own SSE

Putting SSE behind a generic reverse proxy and a shared store is a fine way to start, but at scale you end up building targeted per-topic fan-out, cross-node state synchronization, connection pooling for millions of long-lived streams, and an auth path that survives reconnect storms — exactly the work RevenProx has already done (brokerless mesh, bloom/merkle/vector-clock sync, UUID topic routing, cached JWT auth with a circuit breaker).

### vs. managed pub/sub (Pusher, Ably, Redis pub/sub, ...)

Managed services excel at removing operational burden and are often right for small-to-medium fan-out. The tradeoffs appear at scale and around ownership: pricing is typically per-message, per-connection, or per-peak, so cost grows with your audience; traffic transits a third party; and you inherit their protocol and quotas. Redis pub/sub in particular gives you a broker to run and, being fire-and-forget through a central node, a scaling ceiling. RevenProx trades "someone else runs it" for "you own it, brokerless, at a flat infrastructure cost."

### Honest caveats

- You operate it: RevenProx is self-hosted infrastructure. If you would rather never run a mesh, a managed service is less work.
- SSE is one-way: if you need full-duplex bidirectional messaging, SSE (and RevenProx) is the wrong primitive; use WebSockets.
- It targets very large scale: the brokerless-mesh design earns its keep at high connection counts; a small app may not need it.

---

## FAQ

What is RevenProx? An open-source, high-performance Server-Sent Events (SSE) proxy built in Zig by dotcommoners. It relays long-lived SSE connections to very large numbers of clients using a brokerless NNG mesh for distributed state, UUID topic-based routing for targeted delivery, and JWT authentication. MIT-licensed and self-hostable.

How many concurrent connections can it handle? Architected for 10M+ concurrent connections across 100+ proxy instances. It scales by adding instances to the NNG mesh rather than growing a central broker; Zig's low, predictable per-connection memory makes holding millions of idle-but-open streams practical.

Is it open source? Yes — MIT license, source at github.com/dotcommoners/revenprox.

What is a brokerless NNG mesh? Proxy instances talk to each other directly over NNG (nanomsg-next-gen) to form a peer-to-peer mesh, with no central broker. State is kept consistent with bloom filters, merkle trees, and vector clocks; any instance can accept a publish and route it to subscribers anywhere, so you scale by adding nodes.

SSE vs WebSockets — why SSE? SSE is a simple, HTTP-native, one-way (server-to-client) streaming protocol with built-in auto-reconnect — ideal for fan-out workloads like token streams, dashboards, notifications, and feeds. WebSockets are full-duplex and better for low-latency bidirectional messaging. RevenProx is purpose-built for the one-way, massive-fan-out SSE case; clients send data back over ordinary HTTP requests.

How do I self-host and deploy it? It builds to a single static binary with Zig (e.g. zig build -Doptimize=ReleaseSafe) and runs on your infrastructure. Run instances, they form the NNG mesh, and scale by running more. Docker and Kubernetes guides are in the documentation at docs.dotcommoners.com/revenprox.

How does authentication work? Clients present a JWT, verified via a webhook to your own auth service. Results are cached so reconnect bursts do not hammer your auth service, and a circuit breaker trips if that upstream degrades.

How are events routed? By UUID topic — the mesh routes each event only to instances holding subscribers for that topic, which write it down matching local SSE connections. UUID topics give an unlimited, collision-free namespace.

Is it a managed service? No. It is self-hosted infrastructure with no vendor control plane and no per-message billing.

---

## Glossary

- SSE (Server-Sent Events): HTTP-native protocol for one-way, server-to-client streaming over a single long-lived response, with automatic client reconnection.
- Brokerless mesh: a topology where nodes communicate peer-to-peer with no central message broker.
- NNG: nanomsg-next-gen, the messaging library over which RevenProx instances form their peer-to-peer mesh.
- Bloom filter: a compact, probabilistic structure summarizing which topics an instance holds subscribers for.
- Merkle tree: a tree of hashes that lets instances compare shared state cheaply and reconcile only differing branches.
- Vector clock: a mechanism for ordering distributed updates without a global clock and detecting concurrent changes.
- UUID topic: a universally unique identifier naming a stream; clients subscribe and publishers publish by topic.
- Circuit breaker: a guard that stops calling a failing upstream (here, the auth webhook) once it degrades.

---

## Features

Scale & topology:
- Built for 10M+ concurrent SSE connections across 100+ instances; capacity scales by adding nodes.
- Brokerless NNG mesh — instances peer directly, with no central message broker; any instance can accept a publish and route it to subscribers anywhere.
- Horizontal by construction: adding throughput means adding nodes, with no shared coordinator to over-provision.

State & routing:
- UUID topic routing: every stream is a UUID topic; each event is delivered only to instances holding matching subscribers, over an effectively unlimited namespace.
- Gossip state sync: bloom filters summarize which topics an instance serves, merkle trees reconcile only differing state, and vector clocks order updates without a global clock.
- No wasted fan-out: bloom-filter summaries let instances skip forwarding events that would land nowhere.

Security & resilience:
- Webhook JWT verification against your own auth service — the proxy enforces identity you own.
- Verification caching so reconnect bursts do not hammer the auth service.
- Circuit breaker that trips when the auth upstream degrades, protecting it from a thundering herd.

Runtime & operations:
- Low, predictable per-connection memory from Zig — no garbage collector, manual memory layout.
- Single static binary (e.g. `zig build -Doptimize=ReleaseSafe`); no runtime to install.
- Self-hosted with no vendor control plane; Docker and Kubernetes guides provided; MIT-licensed.

---

## Quickstart

Deploy RevenProx as a brokerless mesh in five steps:

1. Build the binary — clone the repo and run `zig build -Doptimize=ReleaseSafe`, producing `./zig-out/bin/sse-proxy` as a single static binary.
2. Configure the mesh & auth — set each instance's listen address, its NNG peers, and the webhook JWT endpoint (with caching TTL and circuit breaker) in `config/proxy.toml`.
3. Run an instance — `./zig-out/bin/sse-proxy --config config/proxy.toml`; it joins the mesh, peers with the other instances, and begins accepting SSE connections.
4. Subscribe a client — clients speak ordinary SSE; open an `EventSource` (or `curl -N`) against `/events/<uuid-topic>` with a JWT and receive events as they are published.
5. Scale out — run more instances; each peers into the brokerless mesh, so throughput and connection capacity grow horizontally with no central broker to grow with them.

RevenProx is self-hosted with no control plane. Full build, configuration, and Docker/Kubernetes deployment guides are in the documentation.

---
